name: Buuz (Steamed Lamb Dumplings)
steps: Mix lamb with onion and a pinch of salt and pepper.
Fill wrappers, pleat closed, and steam 15 minutes.
steps_detailed: Combine the lamb, onion, garlic, ginger, black pepper, and scallions in a bowl. Mix well with a pinch of salt - the filling should be well-seasoned since it's the whole flavor of the dish.

Place a spoonful of filling in the center of each wrapper. Gather the edges up and pleat closed at the top, leaving a small opening (traditional buuz have a distinctive pleated top, but a simple pinch-closed pouch works fine).
Set the dumplings in a steamer basket lined with parchment or lightly oiled, without touching each other.
Steam over boiling water for 15-18 minutes until the wrapper is translucent and the filling is cooked through.
Serve hot - traditionally eaten by hand, taking a small bite first to release the steam inside.
description: Buuz, steamed dumplings pinched into a distinctive open-top shape, are made in huge batches for Mongolian Lunar New Year (Tsagaan Sar).

name: Khuushuur (Fried Meat Pastries)
steps: Mix beef with onion and seasoning.
Make a simple flour-water dough, roll thin, fill with beef, fold and seal.
Pan-fry 3-4 minutes per side until golden.
steps_detailed: Make a simple dough with the flour and about 1/3 cup water, kneading until smooth. Rest 15 minutes.
Mix the beef with onion, garlic, black pepper, and a pinch of salt.
Divide the dough into small balls and roll each into a thin round, about 5-6 inches across.
Place a spoonful of filling on one half, fold over, and crimp the edges tightly to seal - these fry flat and need a secure seal to hold the filling.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry 3-4 minutes per side until deeply golden and the meat is cooked through.
Drain briefly and serve hot - a Naadam festival classic.
description: Khuushuur, fried meat pastries, a Naadam festival staple.

name: Tsuivan (Stir-Fried Noodles)
steps: Brown the lamb, 5-6 minutes.
Add carrot and cabbage, stir-fry 6-7 minutes.
Toss with cooked noodles.
steps_detailed: Cook the noodles per package directions, drain, and toss with a little oil so they don't stick.
Heat a splash of oil in a wok or wide skillet over high heat. Brown the lamb, breaking it apart, 5-6 minutes.
Add the onion, carrot, and garlic, stir-frying 4-5 minutes.
Add the cabbage and stir-fry 3-4 minutes until just tender but still with some bite.
Add the cooked noodles and soy sauce, tossing everything together over high heat for 2-3 minutes so the noodles pick up a bit of char.
Finish with black pepper and serve immediately - a hearty herder's staple.
description: Tsuivan, hand-pulled noodles stir-fried with meat and vegetables, a Mongolian everyday staple.

name: Khorkhog-Style Lamb Stew
steps: Brown the lamb, 6-7 minutes.
Add carrot, potato, and 1 cup water, simmer 30-35 minutes until tender.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a heavy pot over medium-high heat and brown the lamb on all sides, 6-7 minutes.
Add the onion and garlic, cooking 4-5 minutes until softened.
Add the broth, bay leaf, and black pepper. Bring to a simmer.
Add the carrot and potato, cover, and simmer 30-35 minutes until the lamb and vegetables are fully tender.
Remove the bay leaf. This is a home-style approximation of khorkhog, traditionally cooked with hot stones in a sealed pot out on the steppe.
description: Khorkhog, traditionally cooked with hot stones dropped directly into the pot with the meat, is Mongolia's iconic celebratory dish - a home stovetop version approximates the same slow, moist cooking without the stones.

name: Guriltai Shul (Mongolian Noodle Soup)
steps: Brown the beef, 5-6 minutes.
Add broth and carrot, simmer 10 minutes, then add noodles and cook until tender.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a pot over medium-high heat. Brown the beef, breaking it apart, 5-6 minutes.
Add the onion and garlic, cooking 4-5 minutes until softened.
Add the broth, carrot, and black pepper. Bring to a simmer and cook 10 minutes until the carrot is tender.
Add the noodles and cook per package timing until tender.
Top with scallions before serving.
description: Guriltai shul, a simple noodle soup, common everyday Mongolian fare.

name: Mongolian Beef Stir-Fry
steps: Sear the beef 2-3 minutes, add soy sauce and scallions, toss 1-2 minutes.
steps_detailed: Toss the sliced beef with cornstarch to lightly coat.
Heat oil in a wok or wide skillet over high heat. Sear the beef in a single layer, 2 minutes per side, until browned. Remove.
Add the garlic and ginger to the same pan, cooking 30 seconds.
Stir in the soy sauce and brown sugar, then return the beef to the pan.
Add the scallions and toss everything together over high heat for 1-2 minutes until the sauce clings to the beef.
Serve immediately while the scallions are still bright.
description: A simple beef stir-fry.

name: Grilled Lamb Skewers
steps: Season the lamb with salt and pepper.
Skewer and grill 3-4 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Toss the lamb cubes with oil, garlic, salt, and a generous amount of black pepper - this simple seasoning is traditional, letting the lamb's own flavor lead.
Thread onto skewers.
Grill over high heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until well-charred outside and still juicy inside.
Rest a few minutes before serving.
description: Grilled lamb, reflecting Mongolia's deep pastoral, meat-centered food tradition.

name: Beef and Cabbage Stir-Fry (Mongolian)
steps: Brown the beef, 5-6 minutes.
Add cabbage and carrot, stir-fry 8 minutes.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a wok or wide skillet over high heat. Brown the beef, breaking it apart, 5-6 minutes.
Add the onion and garlic, stir-frying 3-4 minutes.
Add the carrot and cabbage, stir-frying 6-8 minutes until the cabbage is tender but still has some bite.
Season with soy sauce and black pepper before serving.
description: A simple beef and cabbage stir-fry.

name: Bansh Soup (Small Dumpling Soup)
steps: Mix beef with seasoning, form tiny dumplings with wrapper quarters.
Simmer in broth 8-10 minutes.
steps_detailed: Mix the beef with onion, garlic, and black pepper.
Place a small pinch of filling on each wrapper quarter and pinch closed into a tiny dumpling - bansh are meant to be bite-sized, smaller than buuz.
Bring the broth to a simmer. Drop the small dumplings in and cook 8-10 minutes until they float and the filling is cooked through.
Top with scallions and serve - a comforting, simple soup often made for guests.
description: Bansh, small dumplings served in broth, a lighter counterpart to buuz.

name: Mongolian Beef and Rice Bowl
steps: Sear the beef 3-4 minutes, toss with carrot.
Serve over cooked rice.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a skillet over high heat and sear the beef, 3-4 minutes, until browned.
Add the garlic and carrot, stir-frying 3-4 minutes.
Stir in the soy sauce.
Cook the rice per package directions and serve the beef over it, topped with scallions.
description: A simple beef and rice bowl.

name: Lamb and Potato Soup
steps: Brown the lamb, 5 minutes.
Add potato and broth, simmer 20 minutes until tender.
steps_detailed: Heat a splash of oil in a pot over medium-high heat. Brown the lamb, breaking it apart, 5-6 minutes.
Add the onion, carrot, and garlic, cooking 4-5 minutes.
Add the broth and potato, season with black pepper.
Simmer 20-25 minutes until the potato is fully tender.
description: A simple lamb and potato soup.

name: Steamed Beef Dumpling Jar
steps: Prepare buuz-style dumplings (see Buuz recipe), cool.
Pack with shredded cabbage for a portable lunch.
steps_detailed: Prepare and steam beef dumplings as in the Buuz recipe, then cool completely.
Toss the shredded cabbage with rice vinegar for a light, crisp side.
Pack the cooled dumplings and cabbage together, with soy sauce on the side for dipping.
description: A portable version of steamed dumplings.

name: Mongolian Noodle Salad
steps: Toss cooled noodles with sliced beef and carrot.
steps_detailed: Whisk the soy sauce, rice vinegar, and sesame oil together for the dressing.
Toss the cooled noodles, beef, carrot, and cucumber with the dressing.
Chill briefly before serving, or eat right away.
description: A simple noodle salad.

name: Mongolian Milk Porridge
steps: Simmer rice in milk 20-25 minutes until thick and creamy.
steps_detailed: Combine the rice and milk in a saucepan. Bring to a gentle simmer.
Cook, stirring occasionally, 20-25 minutes until the rice is very soft and the mixture has thickened into a porridge - milk-based dishes are central to the nomadic dairy tradition.
Stir in the honey and raisins before serving warm.
description: Dairy is central to Mongolian cooking, reflecting its pastoral, herding tradition.

name: Fried Dough with Cheese (Mongolian)
steps: Make a simple dough, roll thin, fill with cheese, fold.
Pan-fry 2-3 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour with milk and a splash of water into a smooth, firm dough. Rest 10 minutes.
Roll into a thin round, sprinkle the crumbled cheese over half, and fold over to enclose, pressing the edges to seal.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry 2-3 minutes per side until golden and the cheese has softened inside.
description: A simple fried dough with cheese.

name: Aaruul-Style Dried Yogurt Bites
steps: Mix yogurt with honey, spread thin on a lined tray, and dry in a low oven (170F) for 2-3 hours until firm and chewy.
steps_detailed: Whisk the yogurt, honey, and vanilla together.
Spread thinly on a parchment-lined baking sheet.

Dry in a very low oven (170F, or the lowest setting) for 2-3 hours, until firm and chewy but not fully hard - this mimics aaruul, the dried curd snack that's a staple of Mongolian herding life, without needing days of open-air drying.
Break into pieces once cooled and firm.
description: Aaruul, dried curd snacks, a traditional Mongolian way of preserving dairy for the long term.

name: Roasted Salted Nuts (Mongolian Style)
steps: Toast almonds in a dry skillet with salt, 3-4 minutes.
steps_detailed: Toss the almonds with melted butter and salt.
Toast in a dry skillet over medium-low heat, 4-5 minutes, stirring often, until fragrant and lightly golden.
Cool before eating - a simple snack reflecting the importance of nuts and dairy fat in the traditional diet.
description: A simple roasted, salted nut snack.

name: Fried Dumpling Bites (Mongolian)
steps: Fill wrappers with seasoned beef, fold and seal.
Pan-fry 2-3 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Mix the beef with onion and black pepper.
Fill each wrapper, fold in half, and crimp the edges to seal.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry 2-3 minutes per side until golden and crisp.
Serve warm as a quick, hearty snack.
description: A smaller, snack-sized fried dumpling.

name: Mongolian Milk Pudding
steps: Whisk cornstarch into a little cold milk, then combine with remaining milk and honey.
Cook over medium heat, stirring, until thickened, about 8 minutes. Chill.
steps_detailed: Whisk the cornstarch into a splash of the cold milk until smooth, to avoid lumps later.
Combine with the remaining milk, honey, and vanilla in a saucepan.
Cook over medium heat, whisking constantly, 8-10 minutes until thickened to a pudding consistency.
Pour into cups and chill at least 1 hour until set.
Dust with cinnamon before serving - a simple, dairy-forward sweet reflecting the milk-rich tradition.
description: A simple milk pudding, dairy being central to Mongolian food tradition.

name: Honey Fried Dough Bites
steps: Make a simple dough, shape into small pieces, fry until golden.
Drizzle with honey.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour with milk into a smooth, firm dough. Rest 10 minutes.
Shape into small bite-sized pieces, flattened slightly.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry 2-3 minutes per side until golden and puffed.
Drain briefly, then drizzle with honey and dust with cinnamon while still warm.
description: A simple honey-glazed fried dough.
